sentences.text: Yes.
Well, to tell about my brother, I have to tell what happened to the men.
In Hungary, all of the men at the age of 18 had to go into the army.

The men could not go into the army-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, the Jewish men went into the army, but were not given arms, but were taken into mines and road-building and very, very hard work in the beginning.
And-- and they were not taken only at the military age, but they were taken up to the age of 50.
Which meant that we had no men who were around between 18 and 50.
All the men were taken away or practically all of them.
There were one or two, but, I mean, there were for some reasons there were exceptions.
But most of the men were away.
One of the sergeants amused himself and threw down stones on them.
And therefore, he had a finger injured to the-- to the extent that he had to relearn his technique later on completely, because this one finger was completely out of action.
And he never really made it-- really quite made it and most probably that was the main reason.
He-- the group with which he was, and most of the groups of the Hungarian men, were eventually taken out into the Ukraine.
Now, they were taken around about July, August.
And that summer, the summer of '44, was an incredibly hot summer, which I will come back to a number of times.
It was an incredibly hot summer.
And they were taken with all their clothing and all their belongings.
And they marched and were given very little food.
And therefore, they eventually discarded most of their luggage, most of their belongings.
Because they just couldn't cope carrying it and carrying themselves.
And by the time they arrived in September to the Ukraine, it was extremely cold.
So very many starved to death, very many froze to death.
And the rest were put onto minefields and were blown up as guinea pigs and were blown up.
There were practically none, one or two, there who returned from the-- all these groups.
My brother and a friend and some others managed to escape.
And they went to Romania.
Now, Romania was always extremely antisemitic.
But they hated the Germans to such an extent that they allowed these Jews in.
And the Jewish community looked after them.
And until the end of the War, they could stay in Romania and they were protected.
So that that's how my brother escaped and remained alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother, as I won't speak-- I don't think I will speak very much about him.
He-- afterwards, he went back to Hungary.
He eventually came out to Paris where I lived after the war where I met him.
We never had a close relationship.
If anything, we had a rather bad relationship, right from my birth onward.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and we-- he is now living in London.
And I see him very, very little, as little as possible.
Because we have lots of trouble.
He has got a daughter with whom I'm extremely friendly and who I-- I adore.
And she-- and we are very, very close.
But with him, if possible, I avoid any contact with my brother.
So, so much about my brother.
I-- I myself-- then we went with my mother.
We were in this ghetto.
Now, I'm going to tell a story which I am somewhat ashamed of.
But I couldn't help myself.
It's the only time when I did something where I behaved in a not civilized way.
And I am telling this story, because very many people did behave in uncivilized ways through-- through our camp time and everything.
And I do speak about that, too, if I speak-- speak to any group.
But I always tell them also that I also misbehaved at one point.
And it's the following.
We were in the ghetto for seven weeks.
My-- a friend of mine, who trained with me in the same training college, and myself, we collected all the children between two and six.
And we were very lucky, because the weather was nice.
And the synagogue courtyard was within the-- within this ghetto.
And so we took all the children, so that they shouldn't be in these terribly cramped conditions.
And we tried and played with them and did occupied them, so that during the day they shouldn't be so cooped up in rooms where there were often 12 to 14 persons.
As I say, we were lucky we were only six in a room.
We had very little to eat, but we managed somehow.
And I still did some so-called bread bags, which was a side bag with a very long [INAUDIBLE].
It was really a shoulder bag, what today is called a shoulder bag.
But we called it bread bag.
And I sewed in quite a lot of jewels of people-- big chains, golden chains, and various things.
I sewed in for myself also something.
But at the last minute, I took it out and put it on the-- under the roof [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a corner.
It might still be there.
I never had occasion to go up there and have a look.
Because when on the-- when seven weeks later-- we-- we went into the ghetto on the 7th, May 7.
And seven weeks later, we were told to take one piece of luggage and go and-- and we were put into rows.
And we were led out into our very first concentration camp.
Now, rumors came that anybody-- everybody was-- was searched.
And on anybody whom they found jewels was beaten up very badly.
So I took quickly out these two-- a platinum chain and a gold chain, very long.
My grandmother was a-- came from a jeweler's family.
And these were things I brought down from Vienna, I mean, which we brought over from Vienna.
And I took them out.
And as I say, I hid them under the roof.
But I did not-- so I had no jewels.
So we were taken out into a disused motor factory, very, very oily, very dirty motor factory.
By that time, they collected from our town-- our town had 45,000 inhabitants.
I don't know exactly the figures.
But I suppose we were around about 3,500 Jews in town.
And they collected from the whole area all the Jews from the neighboring towns and villages.
And we were 4,850-- 60 I believe is the figure in this first concentration camp on the outskirts of the town.
And we spent there the night.
We went inside.
Some were inside the barracks.
And some were outside on the ground, they slept there.
And in the morning, I heard the loud speaker shouting that 50 people to go to work in another town.
And for some reason or other, I decided we had to be amongst these 50.
My mother didn't want to go.
And I pulled her.
And my mother really never recovered.
My mother was always a bit-- I mean, she was senile from that moment on.
She was really not quite herself.
But she was very, very stubborn.
And she never wanted to move from one place to another.
From the ghetto, she never wanted to go wherever we-- we were supposed to go.
Anyway, I dragged her.
I-- I physically dragged her.
And when we arrived-- and I arrived there, there were 49 people already.
And I was obsessed.
I was absolutely obsessed.
I had to get into those 50.
And I must say, I pushed physically.
I pushed someone out of those, someone I knew, I knew my whole life.
And she was-- but I knew that she was alone.
She was not with someone.
And I physically pushed her out to get into this 50.
And we got into this 50.
And they put us into a train.
And they took us to this neighboring town.
And there they took us into another factory, which was an ex-sugar factory.
The place was called Sarvar, S-A-R-V-A-R.
And then we-- we were-- and we were put into one of the barracks.
There were several barracks.
And each of them was hermetically sealed from the other.
And the first person we came across was my father of whom we had no news for the last 2 1/2 months.
Now, I can't explain it.
But as I say, I was possessed.
I had to get in there.
I still can't explain it what has happened.
But this is something that another-- twice it happened to me that I spoke out.
I was nearly lynched.
I mean, then I didn't do anything, any harm to anyone.
But I-- I spoke out.
And I was nearly lynched.
And I absolutely adamantly said, and it always came true.
And-- and it was just something that happened to me during the war, some-- some extrasensory perception.
I don't know.
I have no idea what it was.
I still can't explain it, but it was something which-- which happened.
As I say then, we were together with my father.
And my father held my hand for the next five days.
That's to say, seven days.
Because two days-- I think we were two days in Sarvar.
I believe so.
I don't know exactly, two days, three days.
I don't know exactly.
And then and-- my mother somehow couldn't take it quite in anymore.
And then on the 2nd of July-- I mean, all this happened at the end of June and beginning of July.
And on the 2nd of July, they told us that they are taking us somewhere else.
And they put us into cattle trucks.
Now, the normal load of cattle trucks was 70 to 90.
They told us that we are going to be special.
And only doctors, solicitors, barristers, architects, and their families will be in that one cattle truck.
And we were 120 in that cattle truck with a bucket.
And in the cattle truck, of course, had, like the usual cattle trucks, tiny little-- two tiny little openings.
We wanted to do it in a civilized way.
And so we put our luggage-- we made up rows of our luggage.
And we sat down back-to-back, knee-to-knee, pulled-up knees.
And that's how we tried to organize ourselves in a civilized way.
And that's how we travelled the next five days and five nights.
The-- obviously, it took some time to accept it that we use a bucket.
Not that we could have used the floor just the same, because the bucket at every jolt, anyway, flowed over.
And we were sitting in everything that was in the bucket.
On the second day in the afternoon, they were shouting that everybody picks up his luggage and get ready to get out.
And we were standing there-- it was still light-- and we were standing there and waiting for the doors to open.
And it got darker and darker.
And when it got really dark, the train started with an extreme jolt.
And we fell over on top of each other.
Now, it was an extremely hot summer, as I said.
We didn't get anything to drink.
And this went on for five days and five nights.
People went mad.
People were screaming.
People collapsed.
People died.
We had a number of death-- dead in our-- amongst us.
And we couldn't empty the buckets.
We couldn't take out the dead.
And that's how it-- how we arrive five days later in Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Birkenau-- well, no, in Auschwitz.
We arrived in-- I think we arrived in Auschwitz, anyway.
Where there was this big gate, we saw that we were arrived at the ramp.
And when we arrived, then they told us to get out very, very quickly.
They pushed us out.
There were people in striped jackets and striped trousers who were pretty-- pushed us out pretty violently.
And then we were separated for men and women.
And that was the last I saw of my father.
He said, well, maybe we can see each other another day.
But I'm afraid that was the last time I saw him.
I believe, there were rumors after the war, but I never could-- could really certify it.
I never could [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ascertain it that he was still seen in East Germany in April, 1945.
But we never heard anything more.
I don't know.
Either he died of starvation, or of typhus, or of-- of a shot, or-- or just-- we don't know.
Anyway, we never heard of him again.
And that was the last time I saw him.
My mother was taken, was dragged, and was practically carried in a direction where there was smoke coming, a pinkish-greyish smoke, and a terrible stench coming from a direction.
And she was taken in with other people in that direction where the older people were taken.
And we found out-- I found out quite soon what the smoke and the stench was about.
And in a way, I was quite happy.
I was pleased for her that it was the end for her, because she wasn't in a state-- she didn't quite understand anymore what was happening.
She completely collapsed in the-- during the transport.
And-- and she didn't know what was happening to her.
So in a way, I was happy.
Because at least she didn't have to go through anything more.
She certainly couldn't have survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was the last I saw my parents.

I myself was taken in a row of five, well, pushed, rushed, hurried, very, very fast, into a very, very cold big hole, where we had to strip naked, where we were shaved by German SS men all our hair on our body, and then disinfected with some very stinging red stuff, and then thrown one piece of clothing.
No underwear, no shoes, just one piece of clothing.
And then we were pushed out.
And when we were all ready, then we were led away in rows of five into another part of the camp.
And we arrived at our destination, which was Birkenau B camp, called also Gypsy camp, which we didn't know at that time.
We didn't know that Gypsies were also persecuted.
And the Gypsy camp-- and that was a Gypsy camp, which was by that time already-- the Gypsies were already eliminated from that camp.
In this camp, there were only barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were about, I don't know how many barracks, and then there was a row of ditches.
And then there was another row of barracks and another row of ditches.
There was a large square, not a tree or a blade of grass to be seen.
Only electric-- electrified wire, barbed wire, German guards with guard dogs and watchtowers to be seen.
Otherwise, nothing.
In the barrack itself, there was nothing.
There was just a soil, the-- well, it was a sand, yellowish-greyish sand.
And that's-- and we fitted in, 1,200 of us, again, the same as we fitted in in the train, back-to-back and knee-to-knee.
And we had latrines, which were guarded by SS men.
Every time we went to the latrines, which we had to do very often.
Because very, very soon, we had dysentery.
Most of us had dysentery.
They were standing there and laughing and making jokes about us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In-- at night, if you wanted to-- I mean, when it got dark, they took us in to the-- put us into the barracks.
And you were supposed to stay in there.
But you were allowed to come out if you needed to go to the latrine.
Except that it wasn't very easy, because since the space was so very tight.
Though, if you gave up your space, you couldn't get back again.
And to get out, you had to climb over bodies.
And if you climbed over bodies, people try to sleep.
It was dark.
They got frightened.
They started to scream.
And if they started to scream, then the Germans would shoot in from outside, into the barracks.
So it was always a very big question, do I go out?
Or, don't I go out?
But you just had to go out.
And then to get in again, there was no way you could get back to your place.
But you had to push somehow or, again, climb over people.
And that was always a terrible thing.
Now, we were-- in the morning, early morning, we were taken out.
We had to go out of the barracks.
And we were not allowed to enter until the evening.
And the temperature was extreme, extremely cold in the-- at night, in early morning.
Then it started to warm up.
As I said, it was a very, very hot summer.
So it became incredibly hot.
And we, with our bare head, and in either a very thick dress or a very thin dress.
So you either froze or you were much too hot.
We, most of us, started to menstruate right at the beginning.
And-- and we had no water to wash ourselves.
We had no underwear.
We could go nowhere.
Everything was sticking to us.
And that was, for me, that was perhaps the most dehumanizing thing of everything, I mean, of-- of my whole time in camp, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the way-- the way  we had to be there.
But it was our last menstruation.
Most of us stopped menstruating after that and during the time we were in camps until the end of the War.
And then for a few months afterwards, we had no more menstruation.
Now--
Well, dehumanizing, because the blood stuck to us.
And because you couldn't wash.
You were filthy.
You were sticky.
You were dirty.
You were sweating.
And the sweat stuck to you.
And you couldn't do anything about it.
You were absolutely powerless.
And if you-- and-- and we had dysentery.
And if you collapsed, then-- then-- then you were taken away.
And that was the last you were heard of.

We knew already, after a couple of days-- many didn't believe it, but I'm afraid I was-- I am quite-- I always faced up to things, so I did believe it-- about the gas chambers and about the crematoria.
And so I-- I-- I didn't want to be taken there.
So anybody who was ill or who felt bad tried to keep on their feet as long as they possibly could.
Because you knew that if you collapsed, that that was the end of you.
In the morning, we were taken out.
And then we had to stand in rows of five.
And we stood for quite a long time, quite a few hours, to be counted.
And then German SS came.
In the first few days, there were German SS who came.
And they-- they counted us.
And they did the counting.
And then we-- and we stood again.
After about three, four days, the German SS women didn't come.
But they were Polish girls, who were about 19, 20 years old, and who came and who were-- who were in charge of us.
Now, these girls were there for the last four, five years.
And most of those who managed to survive, who had such an instinct of survival that they could survive for four or five years.
And they came there when they were 14, 15.
And in these conditions they could survive, they were completely dehumanized.
And that was a terrible thing to-- to see them.
But at the same time, they were so dehumanized that they really were absolutely vicious to us.
They were really very, very bad-- bad the way they treated us.
We had that already when we arrived on the ramp.
We had that already.
That it was quite obvious they were very jealous of us that we still were well-fed, well-dressed.
And they were already completely gone, I mean, and had so many terrible years behind them.
And at that time, I didn't understand it.
Later on, I understood it.
And I mean, I-- I found it-- I mean, I understood what has happened and how dehumanized they became.
Because they had such a strong instinct of survival.
And sadly, there were many who, I mean, most-- most good people-- no, no, I shouldn't say good people.
That's wrong, the wrong expression when I say good people.
Many many didn't survive.
I mean, most people didn't survive.
But those who survived, they had to have such an instinct of survival.
That at that point, very many, most of them, would compromise already and would be willing to do, really, anything.
And they had to be in this position that they-- they were-- they could replace the SS.
They had to show that they are worse than the SS, just to prove it, which was a terrible thing.
And as I say, at the time, I didn't understand it.
And we-- and after-- I mean, at the end of the war, quite a few of these girls who managed to survive were then lynched by Jewish Jews who suffered under them.
I actually met one who was one of the women who looked after us.
And I  met one, and I-- well, I certainly didn't take part in any lynching.
And I met one after the war.
I don't know whether she recognized me, but it was-- but perhaps I come do that a little bit later.
I met her in Paris after the war.
We didn't really speak.
We just were introduced to each other.
And I didn't tell anyone that she was one of these women.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- sorry, I must take a-- [DRINKS]
